title: Share of the population newly infected with a sexually transmitted disease
subtitle: Estimated annual incidence of [trichomoniasis](#dod:trichomoniasis), [chlamydia](#dod:chlamydia), [gonorrhea](#dod:gonorrhea), [genital herpes](#dod:genital-herpes), or [syphilis](#dod:syphilis) each year.
caption:
Share of the population newly infected with a sexually transmitted disease

Estimated annual incidence of trichomoniasis, chlamydia, gonorrhea, genital herpes, or syphilis each year.

This chart presents four horizontal bar charts comparing the estimated annual incidence of five sexually transmitted diseases across different income groups in 2023. The groups are Low-income, Lower-middle-income, Upper-middle-income, and High-income countries. In Low-income countries, Trichomoniasis has the highest incidence at 3%, followed by Chlamydia at 2.3%, Gonorrhea at 1.2%, Genital herpes at 0.7%, and Syphilis at 0.6%. For Lower-middle-income countries, Trichomoniasis leads at 1.9%, with Chlamydia at 1.5%, while Gonorrhea, Genital herpes, and Syphilis are lower at 0.5%, 0.3%, and 0.3% respectively. In Upper-middle-income countries, Trichomoniasis is again the most common at 2.1%, followed closely by Chlamydia at 1.8%; Gonorrhea is at 0.7%, with Genital herpes and Syphilis both at 0.3% and 0.2%. In High-income countries, the order shifts, with Chlamydia having the highest incidence at 1.7%, followed by Trichomoniasis at 1%. The remaining diseases show lower rates: Genital herpes and Gonorrhea are both at 0.2%, and Syphilis is below 0.1%. Across all income groups, Trichomoniasis and Chlamydia consistently show the highest incidence rates, while Syphilis generally shows the lowest. The data source is IHME, Global Burden of Disease (2025).
